critic_raw:
1. The design restricts the patient population to "patients with a history of frequent healthcare utilization," explicitly as a data quality convenience. This introduces a severe selection bias: frequent utilizers have systematically higher baseline readmission risk, more complete record histories, and different comorbidity profiles than the general admitted population. Any model — RNN or logistic regression — trained and evaluated on this population will show inflated performance relative to deployment on all admitted patients. The RNN's advantage may also be specific to this population: frequent utilizers have longer, richer sequence histories that the LSTM can exploit; for typical patients with sparse histories, the RNN advantage likely shrinks or disappears.

2. The preprocessing pipeline is fit on the entire dataset before splitting, as described explicitly in Section 3. The same leakage concerns apply here as in other designs: test-set imputation statistics, scaling parameters, and encoding schemes are informed by the test data. For ICD-10 code vocabularies in particular, fitting a vocabulary on the full dataset means the training model sees codes that appeared only in test-period encounters, and test-period codes are represented in the embedding space with statistics from the full dataset.

3. Section 6 describes "a final check on a subset of the test set" before committing to full training, with the statement: "If the test subset indicates validation overfitting, slight adjustments are made before full test evaluation." This is test-set peeking. Any adjustment conditioned on test subset results invalidates the subsequent test evaluation. The design explicitly endorses this practice under the framing of "ensuring generalization," but it directly corrupts the unbiasedness of the test estimate.

4. The RNN processes "the most recent 50 encounters per patient," but for the restricted frequent-utilizer population, 50 encounters may represent only 1–2 years of history for high utilizers but 5+ years for moderate utilizers. Without normalizing the temporal extent of the sequence (e.g., per-year encounter rates rather than raw encounter counts), the RNN input confounds frequency with duration. A patient with 50 encounters over 2 years has a fundamentally different trajectory pattern than one with 50 encounters over 5 years, but they receive the same-length sequence input.

5. The logistic regression baseline receives "aggregated temporal features" (count of prior admissions, average length of stay, proportion resulting in readmission) while the RNN receives the raw sequence. This means logistic regression has access to a feature directly derived from past readmission outcomes — the proportion of prior admissions resulting in readmission — which is a highly predictive hand-engineered label-correlated feature. If this feature dominates logistic regression performance, the comparison tests "does sequence modeling beat a near-label feature," which is a weaker hypothesis than stated.
all_issues_raised: Population restricted to frequent healthcare utilizers introduces selection bias that inflates performance and makes results non-representative of the full admitted population
Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting injects test-period ICD code distributions into training vocabulary and scaling statistics
Explicit test subset peeking before final evaluation described and endorsed, directly invalidating the unbiased test estimate
Fixed 50-encounter sequence input confounds encounter frequency with temporal duration across the heterogeneous frequent-utilizer population
Logistic regression baseline receives a near-label feature (proportion of prior admissions resulting in readmission) that dominates prediction independently of architecture
Imputation statistics, scaling, and encoding fit on full 5-year dataset before splitting, leaking test period statistics into training preprocessing
Stratified random split on longitudinal EHR data creates patient-level leakage; patients with multiple admissions appear in both training and test sets, benefiting the RNN disproportionately
Section 6 explicitly describes making parameter adjustments after observing test subset performance, constituting test set contamination that invalidates the held-out evaluation
Dataset restricted to frequent healthcare utilizers introduces selection bias; RNN advantage on this population may not transfer to the general discharge population
RNN receives raw temporal sequences while logistic regression receives aggregated summaries; comparison conflates temporal structure exploitation with information compression
Test set subset used for pre-evaluation before model adjustment — explicit test set leakage making final metrics optimistic
Selection bias: dataset restricted to frequent healthcare utilizers — readmission rate and predictive features differ from general admitted population
Preprocessing statistics fit on full dataset before temporal split — missingness pattern information from test period leaks into training imputation
Feature asymmetry: logistic regression receives fewer derived features than what is available from the RNN's raw sequence input — comparison tests information content plus architecture
